text:
Header: Scientific Publication Summary:
Title: Omega-3 fatty acid addition during pregnancy

Middleton P, Gomersall JC, Shepherd E, et al. Omega-3 fatty acid addition during pregnancy. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 2018, Issue 11. Art. No.: CD003402. DOI: 10.1002/14651858.CD003402.pub3.

Background & Rationale
Preterm birth remains a leading cause of perinatal mortality and long-term morbidity in children worldwide. While advances in neonatal care have improved survival rates, preventing preterm labour remains a significant obstetric challenge. Observational data has long suggested a link between omega-3 fatty acid intake and reduced risk of preterm birth, but randomised controlled trial evidence has been inconsistent. This Cochrane systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate whether omega-3 fatty acid supplementation during pregnancy reduces the incidence of preterm birth and other adverse perinatal outcomes.

Study Design
This was a Cochrane systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials. The authors searched multiple databases including CENTRAL, MEDLINE, Embase, and clinical trial registries up to July 2018. Randomised controlled trials comparing omega-3 fatty acid supplementation with placebo or no intervention in pregnant women were included. The primary outcomes were preterm birth before 37 weeks and early preterm birth before 34 weeks. Secondary outcomes included perinatal death, low birthweight, and other neonatal morbidities. Data were pooled using random effects meta-analysis where appropriate, and risk of bias was assessed using Cochrane criteria.

Patient Population
The review included 70 randomised controlled trials involving 19,927 women. Most trials were conducted in high income countries and included women with singleton pregnancies. The mean gestational age at trial entry was around 20 weeks. Women were generally healthy, although some trials included those at increased risk of preterm birth. The majority of trials used fish oil-derived omega-3 supplements, typically providing 1000 mg of EPA and DHA daily. Control groups received either placebo or no supplementation.

Key Findings
Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation was associated with a significant reduction in the risk of preterm birth before 37 weeks (average risk ratio 0.89, 95% CI 0.85 to 0.94; 19,927 women, 70 trials). The effect was more pronounced for early preterm birth before 34 weeks (average risk ratio 0.85, 95% CI 0.76 to 0.94; 19,927 women, 70 trials). There was also a reduction in the risk of low birthweight (average risk ratio 0.83, 95% CI 0.70 to 0.98; 19,927 women, 70 trials). No significant effect was seen on perinatal death, although the confidence intervals were wide. Subgroup analyses suggested the effect was consistent across different omega-3 formulations and doses.

Discussion
The authors concluded that omega-3 fatty acid supplementation during pregnancy reduces the risk of preterm birth and low birthweight. The findings support the potential for omega-3 supplementation as a low-cost intervention to improve perinatal outcomes. The review found no evidence of harm from supplementation. The authors noted that the quality of evidence was moderate to high for most outcomes, though some uncertainty remained around rare outcomes like perinatal death. They suggested that the mechanism may involve anti-inflammatory effects and improved membrane stability.

Authors' Conclusions
The authors concluded that omega-3 fatty acid supplementation during pregnancy reduces the risk of preterm birth before 37 weeks and early preterm birth before 34 weeks, as well as low birthweight. They stated that these findings support the use of omega-3 supplementation as a strategy to reduce preterm birth and improve perinatal outcomes. The authors noted that the evidence was of moderate to high quality for most outcomes assessed.

Reference
Middleton P, Gomersall JC, Shepherd E, et al. Omega-3 fatty acid addition during pregnancy. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 2018, Issue 11. Art. No.: CD003402. DOI: 10.1002/14651858.CD003402.pub3.

This Scientific Publication Summary is an objective summary of the published trial for personal and educational use. It does not constitute clinical advice, endorsement of the intervention, or a recommendation to alter clinical practice.
